title: Red Wine Gravy
subtitle: Pan drippings, red wine, and beef stock reduced to a rich, glossy sauce
excerpt: Red wine gravy is made from the roasting pan juices, a glass of red wine, and hot stock. The wine goes in first and reduces, losing its harshness and concentrating into the pan, before the stock goes in. A little flour stirred into the fat in the pan thickens it as the liquid reduces. It is the gravy that comes from taking the time to deglaze and reduce rather than simply adding stock to the pan juices.
sourceNotes: Classic British Sunday roast gravy made from pan drippings and red wine. Delia Smith, Complete Cookery Course (1978): gravy from roasting pan. Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): red wine gravy method. Standard British roast accompaniment.

content.text: The best gravy comes from the roasting pan, not from a separate saucepan. The browned deposits on the pan base are concentrated flavour;

with wine lifts all of that into the sauce. Tip off most of the fat first, leaving just enough to cook the flour into. The wine goes in before the stock so it can reduce and lose its raw harshness; adding it after the stock gives a wine-forward flavour that never quite integrates.
This recipe assumes roasting pan drippings are available from the joint. If making without a roasting pan, start by browning a tablespoon of butter in a saucepan before adding the flour.
Make the gravy from the pan

Remove the rested joint from the roasting pan. Pour off most of the fat, leaving approximately 2 tablespoons. Place the pan on the hob over a medium heat. Stir in {{plain-flour}} of flour and cook for 1 minute, scraping up any browned bits. Pour in {{red-wine}} of red wine and let it bubble hard for 2 to 3 minutes, still scraping the pan base, until reduced by half. Add {{stock-beef}} of hot stock and stir to combine. Bring to a simmer and cook for 10 minutes until the gravy has thickened to a coating consistency. Season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper}}. Strain through a sieve if a smooth gravy is preferred.

rows.fix: The wine needs to reduce and bubble for at least 2 to 3 minutes before any stock goes in. A harsh wine flavour is a sign it was not reduced sufficiently.
rows.cause: Wine not reduced enough before adding stock
rows.symptom: Gravy tastes strongly of raw wine

rows.fix: Continue simmering uncovered. The gravy will thicken further. Alternatively, mix a teaspoon of flour with a teaspoon of soft butter (beurre manie) and whisk in small pieces.
rows.cause: Too much stock, or insufficient flour
rows.symptom: Gravy too thin after 10 minutes simmering

rows.fix: Add a splash more unseasoned stock or a little water to dilute. Taste before seasoning at the end.
rows.cause: Concentrated roasting juices were already well seasoned

content.text: Red wine gravy is made alongside roast beef, roast lamb, and game. The wine used should be drinkable but does not need to be expensive; the cooking process concentrates the flavour, so a very cheap wine can give a harsh result. The same wine served at the table is a reasonable guide.
